content.text: Review SLAs and Ownership Rules

Service level agreements (SLAs) for code and prompt review are the mechanism that prevents the review process from becoming a bottleneck. Without published SLAs, PRs accumulate in review queues, engineers lose confidence in the process, and critical changes (hotfixes, security patches) compete for attention alongside routine improvements.

This document defines the SLA table for GenAI PR reviews, the ownership rules that determine who is responsible for each review type, the escalation path when SLAs are breached, and the tooling that makes SLA tracking automatic.

attrs.alt: Review SLA timeline showing standard, high-surface, hotfix, and escalation paths

content.text: SLA Table
SLAs are measured in business days from the time the PR is ready for review — meaning all automated gates have passed. A PR that is blocked on a failing CI check does not start the SLA clock.
Review type
First review
Approval / merge
Security review (if required)
Standard (no user content, low surface)
≤ 2 biz days
≤ 4 biz days
N/A
High surface (medium/high injection or user content)
≤ 2 biz days (domain)
≤ 7 biz days (after security review)
≤ 5 biz days
Hotfix
Same business day
Same business day (+ security if flagged)
Same day (on-call security)
Major version bump (breaking change)
≤ 2 biz days
≤ 5 biz days
Required if risk flags set; same security SLA
Bulk baseline update (emergency)
Same business day
Same business day
Pre-approved by security lead

SLAs apply to the reviewer, not the author. If the author does not respond to review comments within 2 business days, the PR is marked stale and the reviewer may close it. The author must re-open with an updated PR when ready.
Ownership Rules
Ownership rules define who is responsible for reviewing a PR and who has final merge authority. These rules are enforced by CODEOWNERS and branch protection — they are not advisory guidelines.
Domain reviewer ownership
Each product area (billing, customer-support, internal-tools) owns a reviewer pool. Membership in the pool requires completing the GenAI reviewer certification and reviewing ≥ 5 PRs under mentorship.

The auto-assignment script picks a random eligible reviewer from the pool, excluding the author. If the assigned reviewer cannot review within 24 hours, they must reassign to another pool member — they cannot simply decline without assigning a replacement.

A reviewer who has been the primary reviewer on ≥ 3 consecutive PRs from the same author must recuse and request a different pool member. Consecutive reviews by the same pair reduce independent scrutiny.
Security reviewer ownership
Security reviewers are members of the security platform team. The pool must have at least 2 members available at any time to maintain the 5-business-day SLA.
Security review is required when injection_surface is medium or high, or contains_user_content is true. This is not a judgment call — the CODEOWNERS file enforces it automatically.

A security reviewer may approve conditionally: they can list required changes and approve 'pending changes'. The author must address all listed items before merge. The security reviewer must re-review only if the changes are substantial — minor wording tweaks do not require re-review.
Tech lead / merge authority
The tech lead (or a designated deputy) has final merge authority. No PR merges without at least one required reviewer approval AND all automated gates green.

The tech lead is responsible for the version pointer update in prompt_versions.py. This is separate from the YAML template review — a tech lead reviews the promotion decision, not just the template content.
The tech lead owns the SLA escalation path: when a domain reviewer does not respond within the SLA, the tech lead intervenes by assigning a backup reviewer from the pool.
Escalation Procedures

SLA breaches should be rare if reviewer pools are adequately staffed and SLAs are realistic. When they do occur, the following procedures ensure the breach is resolved quickly and the root cause is addressed.
Breach event
Trigger
Immediate action
Root cause action
No first domain review within 2 biz days
Automated Slack alert fires
Tech lead pings reviewer pool in #prompt-governance; assigns backup
If ≥ 3 breaches in a week: expand reviewer pool or reduce PR intake
Security review > 5 biz days
PR label 'security-review-overdue' added by CI
Author files P2 security ticket; security lead assigns
Review security team capacity; add reviewer or adjust SLA
Hotfix not reviewed same day
HOTFIX label present; no approval by 17:00
Author pages on-call tech lead directly
Post-mortem on why hotfix wasn't caught pre-production
Author unresponsive to review comments > 2 biz days
Reviewer manually closes PR as stale
PR closed; author notified
Author's manager informed if pattern repeats

# scripts/check_review_slas.py — run daily as a GitHub Actions scheduled job
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from github import Github
import os

g = Github(os.environ["GH_TOKEN"])
repo = g.get_repo(os.environ["REPO"])

# SLA thresholds (business hours are not modelled here — use calendar days for simplicity)
SLA_FIRST_REVIEW_DAYS = 2
SLA_SECURITY_REVIEW_DAYS = 5

now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)

for pr in repo.get_pulls(state="open"):
    if not any(f.filename.startswith("src/prompts/") for f in pr.get_files()):
        continue

    # Has CI passed? (simple proxy: no failing check runs)
    head_commit = repo.get_commit(pr.head.sha)
    ci_failed = any(
        run.conclusion == "failure" for run in head_commit.get_check_runs()
    )
    if ci_failed:
        continue  # SLA clock hasn't started

    ci_pass_time = min(
        (run.completed_at for run in head_commit.get_check_runs()
         if run.conclusion == "success"),
        default=pr.created_at
    )

    days_waiting = (now - ci_pass_time).days
    has_review = any(
        r.state in ("APPROVED", "CHANGES_REQUESTED")
        for r in pr.get_reviews()
    )

    if not has_review and days_waiting > SLA_FIRST_REVIEW_DAYS:
        print(f"SLA BREACH: PR #{pr.number} '{pr.title[:50]}' "
              f"waiting {days_waiting} days for first review")
        # Post a comment to alert the team
        pr.create_issue_comment(
            f"⚠️ Review SLA breached: this PR has been waiting "
            f"{days_waiting} business days for a first review. "
            f"Tech lead: please assign a reviewer from the pool."
        )
        pr.add_to_labels("review-sla-breached")
Reviewer Certification and Pool Management

Review quality depends on reviewer competence, not just availability. The certification process ensures that every domain reviewer understands both the technical and safety dimensions of GenAI prompt review.
Certification requirements
Complete the 'GenAI Prompt Engineering and Safety' training course (4 hours, self-paced).
Pass the reviewer knowledge check (multiple-choice exam on injection vectors, versioning rules, and eval design).
Shadow an experienced reviewer on 3 PRs: observe without voting.
Co-review 2 PRs with an experienced reviewer acting as mentor: cast an independent review, then compare notes.
Complete 1 solo review with no mentor, reviewed retrospectively by the tech lead.
Added to the pool by the tech lead. Certification is valid for 12 months and requires annual renewal.
Pool size guidelines
PR volume (per week)
Minimum pool size
Target pool size
1–5 PRs
2 reviewers
3 reviewers
6–15 PRs
3 reviewers
5 reviewers
5 reviewers
8 reviewers
> 30 PRs
8 reviewers + dedicated triage role
Split into sub-domain pools

Pool size must account for leave, illness, and reviewer burnout. If the pool has fewer than 2 available reviewers at any time (due to leave), the tech lead must either delay non-urgent PRs or temporarily expand the pool by certifying additional reviewers on an emergency basis.
Automating SLA Tracking
Manual SLA tracking does not scale. The following GitHub Actions workflows automate the measurement and alerting so that SLA compliance is visible in the engineering dashboard without manual effort.

# .github/workflows/review-sla-monitor.yml
name: Review SLA Monitor

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 9 * * 1-5"   # 09:00 UTC, Monday–Friday
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  check-slas:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      issues: write
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install PyGithub
      - name: Check review SLAs
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          REPO: ${{ github.repository }}
        run: python scripts/check_review_slas.py
      - name: Post SLA dashboard to Slack
        if: always()
        env:
          SLACK_WEBHOOK: ${{ secrets.SLACK_PROMPT_GOVERNANCE_WEBHOOK }}
        run: python scripts/post_sla_dashboard.py

The dashboard post to Slack shows a weekly summary: number of PRs opened, first-review SLA compliance rate, security review SLA compliance rate, and any currently breached PRs with their owners tagged.
Defining Business Days for SLA Purposes

SLAs expressed in 'business days' require a precise definition. Vague definitions create disputes when a reviewer receives a PR on a Friday afternoon and the team disagrees about whether Monday counts as day 1 or day 2.
Business days are Monday–Friday in the team's primary timezone. Public holidays in that timezone do not count as business days.
The SLA clock starts at the time the last automated gate passes, rounded up to the next business hour (09:00). A PR where CI passes at 18:45 on Monday starts its clock at 09:00 Tuesday.

The SLA clock stops when a reviewer posts a substantive review comment or approves. A comment that only says 'LGTM' without checking the review checklist does not stop the clock — it must be a real review.
For distributed teams spanning multiple timezones, use UTC 09:00 as the day boundary. A PR open at 08:55 UTC rounds to the same day; one open at 09:05 UTC starts the next business day.

# scripts/business_day_utils.py
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

# Public holidays (example: UK 2025) — extend per team timezone
UK_HOLIDAYS_2025 = {
    datetime(2025, 1, 1).date(),   # New Year
    datetime(2025, 4, 18).date(),  # Good Friday
    datetime(2025, 4, 21).date(),  # Easter Monday
    datetime(2025, 5, 5).date(),   # May Day
    datetime(2025, 5, 26).date(),  # Spring Bank Holiday
    datetime(2025, 8, 25).date(),  # Summer Bank Holiday
    datetime(2025, 12, 25).date(), # Christmas
    datetime(2025, 12, 26).date(), # Boxing Day
}

BUSINESS_START_HOUR = 9   # 09:00 UTC
BUSINESS_END_HOUR   = 17  # 17:00 UTC

def next_business_open(dt: datetime) -> datetime:
    dt = dt.replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc)
    # Round up to next business open
    candidate = dt.replace(hour=BUSINESS_START_HOUR, minute=0, second=0, microsecond=0)
    if dt.hour >= BUSINESS_START_HOUR:
        candidate += timedelta(days=1)
    while candidate.weekday() >= 5 or candidate.date() in UK_HOLIDAYS_2025:
        candidate += timedelta(days=1)
    return candidate

def business_days_elapsed(start: datetime, end: datetime) -> int:
    start = next_business_open(start)
    days = 0
    current = start
    while current <= end:
        if current.weekday() < 5 and current.date() not in UK_HOLIDAYS_2025:
            days += 1
        current += timedelta(days=1)
    return days
Feedback Loop: Using SLA Data to Improve the Process
SLA metrics are not just a compliance report — they are diagnostic data about process health. The following analysis patterns identify structural problems that individual escalations cannot fix.
Pattern 1: Clustered SLA breaches

If SLA breaches cluster around a specific day of the week (e.g. Friday afternoon PRs consistently breach the Monday first-review SLA), the fix is a policy change: do not open prompt PRs on Friday afternoons, or adjust the SLA to exclude weekends explicitly.
Pattern 2: SLA compliance drops after team headcount change

A drop in SLA compliance following a team member leaving or moving teams signals that the reviewer pool was already at the minimum — one fewer reviewer broke the SLA budget. The fix is pool expansion, not escalation.
Pattern 3: Security review is the consistent bottleneck
If security review SLA is consistently breached while domain review is healthy, the bottleneck is in the security team, not the product teams. Options:
Add a second trained security reviewer to the pool.
Reduce the number of templates that require security review by improving template design (avoid user content where not needed; use lower injection_surface designs).
Pre-approve low-risk medium-surface templates with a light-touch security review instead of the full protocol.

# Weekly SLA analysis script — find patterns in breach data
import json
from collections import defaultdict
from datetime import datetime

def analyse_breaches(breach_log: list[dict]) -> dict:
    by_day_of_week = defaultdict(int)
    by_review_type = defaultdict(int)
    by_team = defaultdict(int)
    by_month = defaultdict(int)

    for breach in breach_log:
        pr_opened = datetime.fromisoformat(breach["pr_opened_at"])
        by_day_of_week[pr_opened.strftime("%A")] += 1
        by_review_type[breach["review_type"]] += 1
        by_team[breach["domain"]] += 1
        by_month[pr_opened.strftime("%Y-%m")] += 1

    return {
        "by_day_of_week": dict(sorted(by_day_of_week.items(),
                                       key=lambda x: ["Mon","Tue","Wed","Thu","Fri","Sat","Sun"].index(x[0][:3]))),
        "by_review_type": dict(sorted(by_review_type.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])),
        "by_team":         dict(sorted(by_team.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])),
        "trend_by_month":  dict(sorted(by_month.items())),
    }

# Example output analysis
report = analyse_breaches(json.loads(open("breach-log.json").read()))
print(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
# {
#   "by_day_of_week": {"Monday": 2, "Tuesday": 1, "Friday": 8},
#   ^^^ Friday spike → policy: no prompt PRs opened Fri 15:00+
#   "by_review_type": {"security-review": 12, "domain-review": 3},
#   ^^^ security bottleneck → expand security reviewer pool
# }
Integrating SLAs into Team Rituals
SLAs only improve if they are reviewed regularly. The following cadence integrates SLA data into existing engineering rituals without adding new meetings.
Ritual
Cadence
SLA data used
Action
Weekly team standup
Weekly
Number of open PRs past SLA; any current breaches
Tech lead assigns backup reviewer for breached PRs on the spot
Engineering manager 1:1
Bi-weekly
SLA compliance rate trend (4-week rolling)
If below target: discuss pool capacity or SLA realism
Prompt governance retro
Monthly
Full breach log, pattern analysis, pool roster
Decide on pool size changes, SLA adjustments, or policy fixes
Security team check-in
Monthly
Security review SLA compliance rate
Flag if security review is bottlenecked; agree on action
Quarterly engineering review
Quarterly
Year-to-date SLA compliance, reviewer pool health
Set targets for next quarter; publish to engineering scorecard

Publish the SLA compliance rate in the team's engineering scorecard alongside other quality metrics (test coverage, incident count, deployment frequency). Visibility creates accountability without requiring additional process.
Summary
SLAs are measured from when automated gates pass, not from PR open. Standard first review is ≤ 2 biz days; security review ≤ 5 biz days; hotfixes same day.
Ownership is enforced by CODEOWNERS and branch protection — not voluntary. Domain reviewer pools assign a random eligible reviewer on PR open.
SLA breach triggers an automated Slack alert and a tech lead assignment of a backup reviewer. Repeated breaches trigger pool size review.
Reviewer certification requires 4-hour training, a knowledge check, and a five-step supervised review progression before being added to the pool.
Pool size must be at least 2× the minimum to absorb leave and illness. For > 30 PRs/week, split into sub-domain pools.
SLA tracking is automated via a daily GitHub Actions job that checks open prompt-related PRs, adds breach labels, and posts a weekly dashboard to Slack.
